1:
"Then sang Deborah and Barak the son of Abinoam on that day, saying," The witnesses note Deborah and Barak singing on the day of victory, Deborah named first. The witnesses note that Gill notes Deborah is named first, being the root of the work, chief both in directing the war and composing the song. The witnesses note that they sang on that day. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the then sang — deborah and Barak; the Deborah first — the chief in the work; the on that day — of victory; the deliverance into praise — i will sing unto the LORD (Exod. 15:1).

The witnesses agree the victory was crowned with a song of praise — "then sang Deborah and Barak... on that day": the deliverance was met not with mere rejoicing but with a solemn song ascribing all to the LORD; Gill notes Deborah, the chief in the work, led the praise; the answering of God's salvation with thanksgiving shows the right use of his mercies; it teaches that the people of God should turn their deliverances into praise, singing to him who saves, as Moses sang at the sea and the redeemed sing the song of the Lamb, "I will sing unto the LORD, for he hath triumphed gloriously" (Exod. 15:1), Deborah and Barak singing on the day of victory.

So the witnesses make the verse then sang they: The witnesses note the then sang: Deborah and Barak. The witnesses note the Deborah first: the chief in the work. The witnesses note the on that day: of victory. The witnesses note the deliverance into praise: I will sing unto the LORD (Exod. 15:1). The expositors observe the then sang: Deborah and Barak. The expositors observe the Deborah first: the chief in the work. The expositors observe the on that day: of victory. The expositors observe the deliverance into praise: I will sing unto the LORD (Exod. 15:1). The believer is taught to mark the then sang, the Deborah first, the on that day, the deliverance into praise, and to praise the LORD for his salvation. The verse thus records that Deborah and Barak sang on the day of victory — the deliverance answered with a song ascribing all to the LORD, the people turning their mercies into praise, I will sing unto the LORD, for he hath triumphed gloriously (Exod. 15:1).
2:
"Praise ye the LORD for the avenging of Israel, when the people willingly offered themselves." The witnesses note the call to praise the LORD for avenging Israel, the people willingly offering themselves. The witnesses note that Gill notes the avenging of the injuries done to Israel by their enemies, particularly Jabin for twenty years. The witnesses note that the people willingly offered themselves. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the praise the LORD — for the avenging; the of Israel — from their foes; the willingly offered — the people's free service; the willing in his day — willing in the day of thy power (Ps. 110:3).

The witnesses agree the song opens with praise for two things — God's avenging of Israel and the people's willing service: "Praise ye the LORD for the avenging of Israel, when the people willingly offered themselves"; the deliverance was God's work, yet the people's ready volunteering was the means he honoured; the joining of God's grace and the people's willingness teaches that the LORD is to be praised both for his salvation and for the willing hearts he gives his servants; it bids us bless him for the free and ready spirit in his cause, "thy people shall be willing in the day of thy power" (Ps. 110:3), praise for the avenging and the willing offering.

So the witnesses make the verse willingly offered: The witnesses note the praise the LORD: for the avenging. The witnesses note the of Israel: from their foes. The witnesses note the willingly offered: the people's free service. The witnesses note the willing in his day: willing in the day of thy power (Ps. 110:3). The expositors observe the praise the LORD: for the avenging. The expositors observe the of Israel: from their foes. The expositors observe the willingly offered: the people's free service. The expositors observe the willing in his day: willing in the day of thy power (Ps. 110:3). The believer is taught to mark the praise the LORD, the of Israel, the willingly offered, the willing in his day, and to praise the LORD for his salvation. The verse thus records that the song praises the LORD for avenging Israel and for the people's willing offering — God to be praised both for his salvation and for the willing hearts he gives, thy people shall be willing in the day of thy power (Ps. 110:3).
3:
"Hear, O ye kings; give ear, O ye princes; I, even I, will sing unto the LORD; I will sing praise to the LORD God of Israel." The witnesses note Deborah summoning kings and princes to hear her praise of the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes she desires that all potentates, far and near, then and in succeeding ages, might hear of the wonderful works of God for his people. The witnesses note that she would sing unto the LORD God of Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the hear, O ye kings — the great ones called; the give ear, O princes — to attend; the I will sing — her resolve; the praise published to kings — i will speak before kings (Ps. 119:46).

The witnesses agree Deborah calls the great ones of the earth to hear her praise — "Hear, O ye kings... I, even I, will sing unto the LORD": she would have the rulers of the nations know the mighty works of Israel's God; the summoning of kings to attend the praise of the LORD shows that his glory is to be published to the highest; the doubled "I, even I" marks the earnestness of her own resolve to sing; it teaches that the people of God should make his praise known before the world, not ashamed to publish his works to kings, "I will speak of thy testimonies also before kings, and will not be ashamed" (Ps. 119:46), Deborah calling kings to hear her song.

So the witnesses make the verse hear, O ye kings: The witnesses note the hear, O ye kings: the great ones called. The witnesses note the give ear, O princes: to attend. The witnesses note the I will sing: her resolve. The witnesses note the praise published to kings: I will speak before kings (Ps. 119:46). The expositors observe the hear, O ye kings: the great ones called. The expositors observe the give ear, O princes: to attend. The expositors observe the I will sing: her resolve. The expositors observe the praise published to kings: I will speak before kings (Ps. 119:46). The believer is taught to mark the hear, O ye kings, the give ear, O princes, the I will sing, the praise published to kings, and to praise the LORD for his salvation. The verse thus records that Deborah called kings and princes to hear her praise of the LORD — his glory published to the highest, the people of God not ashamed to make his works known before kings, speaking of his testimonies before kings (Ps. 119:46).
4:
"LORD, when thou wentest out of Seir, when thou marchedst out of the field of Edom, the earth trembled, and the heavens dropped, the clouds also dropped water." The witnesses note the song recalling God's majestic coming for his people, the earth trembling at his march. The witnesses note that Gill notes here properly begins the song, with an apostrophe to the Lord, recalling ancient appearances of God for his people. The witnesses note that the earth trembled and the heavens dropped. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thou wentest out of Seir — god's coming; the the earth trembled — at his march; the the heavens dropped — nature moved; the majesty in deliverance — the mountains quaked at his presence (Nah. 1:5).

The witnesses agree the song rises to the majesty of God's coming for his people — "LORD, when thou wentest out of Seir... the earth trembled, and the heavens dropped": the imagery recalls his terrible glory at Sinai and in the leading of Israel, when all nature shook before him; the trembling earth and dropping heavens magnify the awful power of the God who fights for his own; it teaches that the LORD comes in majesty to deliver his people, the whole creation moved at his approach, "the mountains quaked at the presence of the LORD" (Nah. 1:5); the God who once shook Sinai is the God who delivered Israel from Sisera, the earth trembling at his march.

So the witnesses make the verse the earth trembled: The witnesses note the thou wentest out of Seir: God's coming. The witnesses note the the earth trembled: at his march. The witnesses note the the heavens dropped: nature moved. The witnesses note the majesty in deliverance: the mountains quaked at his presence (Nah. 1:5). The expositors observe the thou wentest out of Seir: God's coming. The expositors observe the the earth trembled: at his march. The expositors observe the the heavens dropped: nature moved. The expositors observe the majesty in deliverance: the mountains quaked at his presence (Nah. 1:5). The believer is taught to mark the thou wentest out of Seir, the the earth trembled, the the heavens dropped, the majesty in deliverance, and to praise the LORD for his salvation. The verse thus records that the song recalls God's majestic march, the earth trembling and heavens dropping — the LORD coming in majesty to deliver his people, the whole creation moved at his approach, the mountains quaking at his presence (Nah. 1:5).
5:
"The mountains melted from before the LORD, even that Sinai from before the LORD God of Israel." The witnesses note the mountains, even Sinai, melting before the LORD God of Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes the inhabitants of the mountains melted through fear, the Lord going before Israel in a pillar of cloud and fire. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the mountains melting before the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the mountains melted — before the LORD; the even that Sinai — where he descended; the men's hearts melting — through fear; the none stands before him — the mountains flowed down (Isa. 64:1).

The witnesses agree the majesty of God is heightened — "the mountains melted from before the LORD, even that Sinai": the very hills, and Sinai itself where he once descended in fire, are pictured as melting before his presence; whether the literal quaking of the mountains or the melting of men's hearts through fear, the image proclaims that nothing can stand before the LORD; it teaches that the greatest and most stable things give way before God's glory, the mountains flowing down at his coming, "the mountains flowed down at thy presence" (Isa. 64:1); the God of Sinai, before whom mountains melt, is the deliverer of his people, even Sinai melting before the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse the mountains melted: The witnesses note the the mountains melted: before the LORD. The witnesses note the even that Sinai: where he descended. The witnesses note the men's hearts melting: through fear. The witnesses note the none stands before him: the mountains flowed down (Isa. 64:1). The expositors observe the the mountains melted: before the LORD. The expositors observe the even that Sinai: where he descended. The expositors observe the men's hearts melting: through fear. The expositors observe the none stands before him: the mountains flowed down (Isa. 64:1). The believer is taught to mark the the mountains melted, the even that Sinai, the men's hearts melting, the none stands before him, and to praise the LORD for his salvation. The verse thus records that the mountains, even Sinai, melted before the LORD — nothing standing before his glory, the greatest things giving way at his coming, the mountains flowing down at his presence (Isa. 64:1).
6:
"In the days of Shamgar the son of Anath, in the days of Jael, the highways were unoccupied, and the travellers walked through byways." The witnesses note the desolation under oppression — the highways deserted, travellers forced to byways. The witnesses note that Gill notes that during the twenty years of Jabin's oppression, robbers made the highways dangerous, so men travelled by crooked paths. The witnesses note that the highways were unoccupied. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the days of Shamgar — and Jael; the the highways unoccupied — deserted; the travellers in byways — by hidden paths; the the misery of bondage — the ways of Zion mourn (Lam. 1:4).

The witnesses agree the song now recalls the misery of the oppression — "the highways were unoccupied, and the travellers walked through byways": such was the terror under Jabin and his robbers that the open roads were deserted and men crept by hidden paths; the picture of desolation shows the wretchedness that sin and bondage brought upon the land; it teaches that the forsaking of God brings danger, fear, and the loss of peace, the ways of the land made unsafe by the enemy God suffered to oppress them, "the ways of Zion do mourn... her gates are desolate" (Lam. 1:4), the highways unoccupied in the days of oppression.

So the witnesses make the verse the highways unoccupied: The witnesses note the the days of Shamgar: and Jael. The witnesses note the the highways unoccupied: deserted. The witnesses note the travellers in byways: by hidden paths. The witnesses note the the misery of bondage: the ways of Zion mourn (Lam. 1:4). The expositors observe the the days of Shamgar: and Jael. The expositors observe the the highways unoccupied: deserted. The expositors observe the travellers in byways: by hidden paths. The expositors observe the the misery of bondage: the ways of Zion mourn (Lam. 1:4). The believer is taught to mark the the days of Shamgar, the the highways unoccupied, the travellers in byways, the the misery of bondage, and to praise the LORD for his salvation. The verse thus records that the highways were unoccupied and travellers forced to byways — the desolation of oppression, the forsaking of God bringing danger and the loss of peace, the ways of Zion mourning, her gates desolate (Lam. 1:4).
7:
"The inhabitants of the villages ceased, they ceased in Israel, until that I Deborah arose, that I arose a mother in Israel." The witnesses note the villages laid waste until Deborah arose as a mother in Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Canaanite robbers entered the villages and unwalled towns, plundering houses, so the villages ceased. The witnesses note that Deborah arose a mother in Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the villages ceased — forsaken; the until I Deborah arose — god's deliverer; the a mother in Israel — her tender care; the comfort in the dark — as one whom his mother comforteth (Isa. 66:13).

The witnesses agree the depth of the misery is marked — "the inhabitants of the villages ceased... until that I Deborah arose, that I arose a mother in Israel": the open towns were so harried that they were forsaken, till God raised up Deborah; her calling herself "a mother in Israel" shows the tender, nurturing care with which she rose to deliver and guide her people; it teaches that God raises up deliverers in the darkest times, and that even a woman may be made a mother to his people, nursing and saving them, "as one whom his mother comforteth" (Isa. 66:13) — Deborah arising a mother in Israel when the villages had ceased.

So the witnesses make the verse a mother in Israel: The witnesses note the the villages ceased: forsaken. The witnesses note the until I Deborah arose: God's deliverer. The witnesses note the a mother in Israel: her tender care. The witnesses note the comfort in the dark: as one whom his mother comforteth (Isa. 66:13). The expositors observe the the villages ceased: forsaken. The expositors observe the until I Deborah arose: God's deliverer. The expositors observe the a mother in Israel: her tender care. The expositors observe the comfort in the dark: as one whom his mother comforteth (Isa. 66:13). The believer is taught to mark the the villages ceased, the until I Deborah arose, the a mother in Israel, the comfort in the dark, and to praise the LORD for his salvation. The verse thus records that the villages ceased until Deborah arose a mother in Israel — God raising up a deliverer in the darkest time, even a woman made a mother to nurse and save his people, as one whom his mother comforteth (Isa. 66:13).
8:
"They chose new gods; then was war in the gates: was there a shield or spear seen among forty thousand in Israel?" The witnesses note Israel choosing new gods, war coming to their gates, and their helpless want of arms. The witnesses note that Gill notes Israel chose new gods, and this refers to their idolatry, after which war came. The witnesses note that there was not a shield or spear among forty thousand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the they chose new gods — the idolatry; the war in the gates — the calamity; the no shield or spear — the people disarmed; the apostasy the cause — he gave his people to the sword (Ps. 78:58-62).

The witnesses agree the song names the root of the misery — "they chose new gods; then was war in the gates": the idolatry of Israel brought the war and the helplessness, so that scarce "a shield or spear" was found among forty thousand, the people disarmed and crushed by their oppressor; the linking of the chosen idols to the war at the gates shows that apostasy is the cause of national calamity; it teaches that the forsaking of God strips his people of strength and leaves them defenceless before their enemies, "they provoked him to anger... therefore he... gave his people over... to the sword" (cf. Ps. 78:58-62), the new gods bringing war and the want of weapons.

So the witnesses make the verse they chose new gods: The witnesses note the they chose new gods: the idolatry. The witnesses note the war in the gates: the calamity. The witnesses note the no shield or spear: the people disarmed. The witnesses note the apostasy the cause: he gave his people to the sword (Ps. 78:58-62). The expositors observe the they chose new gods: the idolatry. The expositors observe the war in the gates: the calamity. The expositors observe the no shield or spear: the people disarmed. The expositors observe the apostasy the cause: he gave his people to the sword (Ps. 78:58-62). The believer is taught to mark the they chose new gods, the war in the gates, the no shield or spear, the apostasy the cause, and to praise the LORD for his salvation. The verse thus records that Israel chose new gods and so war came to their gates, leaving them disarmed — apostasy the cause of national calamity, the forsaking of God stripping his people of strength, given over to the sword (Ps. 78:58-62).
9:
"My heart is toward the governors of Israel, that offered themselves willingly among the people. Bless ye the LORD." The witnesses note Deborah's heart drawn to the rulers who offered themselves willingly, and her call to bless the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes most Jewish commentators interpret this of the wise men and scribes willing to teach the people the law even in trouble. The witnesses note that she calls to bless the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the my heart is toward — the governors; the offered themselves willingly — the free givers; the honoured and loved — dear to the people; the bless ye the LORD — of thine own have we given (1 Chr. 29:14).

The witnesses agree Deborah's heart went out to those who freely gave themselves to the cause — "my heart is toward the governors of Israel, that offered themselves willingly": the leaders who, in the day of danger, ventured all for God and their people are honoured with her love and praise; the joining of "Bless ye the LORD" shows that even their willingness was a gift to be ascribed to him; it teaches that the willing servants of God are dear to his people and worthy of honour, and that their readiness is itself cause to bless the LORD who gave it, "of thine own have we given thee" (1 Chr. 29:14), Deborah's heart toward the willing governors.

So the witnesses make the verse the willing governors: The witnesses note the my heart is toward: the governors. The witnesses note the offered themselves willingly: the free givers. The witnesses note the honoured and loved: dear to the people. The witnesses note the bless ye the LORD: of thine own have we given (1 Chr. 29:14). The expositors observe the my heart is toward: the governors. The expositors observe the offered themselves willingly: the free givers. The expositors observe the honoured and loved: dear to the people. The expositors observe the bless ye the LORD: of thine own have we given (1 Chr. 29:14). The believer is taught to mark the my heart is toward, the offered themselves willingly, the honoured and loved, the bless ye the LORD, and to praise the LORD for his salvation. The verse thus records that Deborah's heart went out to the rulers who offered themselves willingly, with a call to bless the LORD — the willing servants of God honoured, their readiness itself his gift to be praised, of thine own have we given thee (1 Chr. 29:14).
10:
"Speak, ye that ride on white asses, ye that sit in judgment, and walk by the way." The witnesses note the call to all ranks — the noble, the judges, the travellers — to tell of God's deliverance. The witnesses note that Gill notes in Judea it was honourable to ride on asses, especially white ones, used by persons of distinction. The witnesses note that those who sit in judgment and walk by the way are called. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ride on white asses — the noble; the sit in judgment — the rulers; the walk by the way — the travellers; the all owe him praise — let every thing that hath breath praise (Ps. 150:6).

The witnesses agree the song summons every rank to join the praise — "ye that ride on white asses" (the noble), "ye that sit in judgment" (the rulers), and they that "walk by the way" (the common travellers): all alike, high and low, are called to tell of the LORD's salvation; the gathering of every sort into the praise shows that God's mercies concern the whole people; it teaches that all ranks of God's people owe him praise and should publish his deliverance, none excused, "let every thing that hath breath praise the LORD" (Ps. 150:6), the noble, the judge, and the wayfarer all called to speak.

So the witnesses make the verse all ranks called: The witnesses note the ride on white asses: the noble. The witnesses note the sit in judgment: the rulers. The witnesses note the walk by the way: the travellers. The witnesses note the all owe him praise: let every thing that hath breath praise (Ps. 150:6). The expositors observe the ride on white asses: the noble. The expositors observe the sit in judgment: the rulers. The expositors observe the walk by the way: the travellers. The expositors observe the all owe him praise: let every thing that hath breath praise (Ps. 150:6). The believer is taught to mark the ride on white asses, the sit in judgment, the walk by the way, the all owe him praise, and to praise the LORD for his salvation. The verse thus records that the song calls every rank — the noble, the judge, the wayfarer — to tell of God's deliverance — all owing him praise, his mercies concerning the whole people, let every thing that hath breath praise the LORD (Ps. 150:6).
11:
"They that are delivered from the noise of archers in the places of drawing water, there shall they rehearse the righteous acts of the LORD..." The witnesses note that those delivered from the enemy would rehearse the righteous acts of the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes the army delivered from the archers of Sisera at the river Kishon, or maidens delivered at the wells. The witnesses note that there they would rehearse the righteous acts of the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the delivered from the archers — the danger past; the the places of drawing water — daily life; the there shall they rehearse — the recounting; the the righteous acts of the LORD — what work thou didst (Ps. 44:1).

The witnesses agree the song points to the glad recounting of God's deeds — "there shall they rehearse the righteous acts of the LORD": those once endangered at the very wells, now delivered, would gather to tell over the saving works of God; the rehearsing of his righteous acts at the watering-places shows how deliverance turns daily life into a theatre of praise; it teaches that the people of God are to recount and rehearse his mighty and righteous deeds, keeping the memory of his salvation alive, "we have heard with our ears... what work thou didst in their days" (Ps. 44:1); the delivered shall rehearse the righteous acts of the LORD, and so his praise abide.

So the witnesses make the verse rehearse his acts: The witnesses note the delivered from the archers: the danger past. The witnesses note the the places of drawing water: daily life. The witnesses note the there shall they rehearse: the recounting. The witnesses note the the righteous acts of the LORD: what work thou didst (Ps. 44:1). The expositors observe the delivered from the archers: the danger past. The expositors observe the the places of drawing water: daily life. The expositors observe the there shall they rehearse: the recounting. The expositors observe the the righteous acts of the LORD: what work thou didst (Ps. 44:1). The believer is taught to mark the delivered from the archers, the the places of drawing water, the there shall they rehearse, the the righteous acts of the LORD, and to praise the LORD for his salvation. The verse thus records that the delivered would rehearse the righteous acts of the LORD at the wells — deliverance turning daily life into a theatre of praise, the people recounting his mighty deeds, the memory of his salvation kept alive, what work thou didst in their days (Ps. 44:1).
12:
"Awake, awake, Deborah: awake, awake, utter a song: arise, Barak, and lead thy captivity captive, thou son of Abinoam." The witnesses note the call to Deborah to awake and sing, and to Barak to lead his captives. The witnesses note that Gill notes she arouses herself to attend the service with more ardour and zeal, or finding herself more impelled by the Spirit. The witnesses note that Barak is called to lead his captivity captive. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the awake, awake — rouse the soul; the utter a song — to praise; the arise, Barak — to triumph; the lead captivity captive — as Christ led captivity captive (Eph. 4:8).

The witnesses agree the song calls itself to fresh ardour — "Awake, awake, Deborah... utter a song: arise, Barak, and lead thy captivity captive": the stirring-up of the soul to praise and the summons to Barak to lead his captives in triumph mark the joy of victory; the phrase "lead thy captivity captive" is taken up by the apostle of Christ's great triumph, who "led captivity captive, and gave gifts unto men" (Eph. 4:8); it teaches that the people of God are to rouse themselves to wholehearted praise, and that Barak's triumph foreshadows the greater triumph of Christ over all his foes, "Awake, awake" the call to bless the LORD, leading captivity captive.

So the witnesses make the verse awake and sing: The witnesses note the awake, awake: rouse the soul. The witnesses note the utter a song: to praise. The witnesses note the arise, Barak: to triumph. The witnesses note the lead captivity captive: as Christ led captivity captive (Eph. 4:8). The expositors observe the awake, awake: rouse the soul. The expositors observe the utter a song: to praise. The expositors observe the arise, Barak: to triumph. The expositors observe the lead captivity captive: as Christ led captivity captive (Eph. 4:8). The believer is taught to mark the awake, awake, the utter a song, the arise, Barak, the lead captivity captive, and to offer himself willingly in the LORD's cause. The verse thus records that the song calls Deborah to awake and Barak to lead his captives in triumph — the soul roused to wholehearted praise, Barak's triumph foreshadowing Christ who led captivity captive and gave gifts unto men (Eph. 4:8).
13:
"Then he made him that remaineth have dominion over the nobles among the people: the LORD made me have dominion over the mighty." The witnesses note the remnant of Israel raised to dominion over the mighty, by the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes the few mean and miserable that remained under Jabin's yoke were raised to a high estate and dominion. The witnesses note that the LORD made them have dominion over the mighty. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he made the remnant — have dominion; the over the nobles — the proud conquerors; the the LORD made me — the reversal his; the the low exalted — he raiseth the poor to set among princes (1 Sam. 2:8).

The witnesses agree the song marvels at the reversal God wrought — "he made him that remaineth have dominion over the nobles... the LORD made me have dominion over the mighty": the poor remnant, lately crushed under Jabin, were now lifted to rule over their proud conquerors; the lifting of the low above the mighty shows the hand of God who exalts the humble; it teaches that the LORD raises his abased people and gives them victory over the great, the weak made to triumph over the strong, "he raiseth up the poor out of the dust... to set them among princes" (1 Sam. 2:8), the remnant given dominion over the mighty.

So the witnesses make the verse dominion over the mighty: The witnesses note the he made the remnant: have dominion. The witnesses note the over the nobles: the proud conquerors. The witnesses note the the LORD made me: the reversal his. The witnesses note the the low exalted: he raiseth the poor to set among princes (1 Sam. 2:8). The expositors observe the he made the remnant: have dominion. The expositors observe the over the nobles: the proud conquerors. The expositors observe the the LORD made me: the reversal his. The expositors observe the the low exalted: he raiseth the poor to set among princes (1 Sam. 2:8). The believer is taught to mark the he made the remnant, the over the nobles, the the LORD made me, the the low exalted, and to offer himself willingly in the LORD's cause. The verse thus records that the crushed remnant was raised to dominion over the mighty by the LORD — the hand of God exalting the humble, the weak made to triumph over the strong, raising the poor out of the dust to set among princes (1 Sam. 2:8).
14:
"Out of Ephraim was there a root of them against Amalek; after thee, Benjamin, among thy people; out of Machir came down governors, and out of Zebulun they that handle the pen of the writer." The witnesses note the song naming the tribes that came to help — Ephraim, Benjamin, Machir, Zebulun. The witnesses note that Gill notes Deborah here mentions the tribes that sided and assisted, beginning with Ephraim, where she dwelt. The witnesses note that out of Zebulun came they that handle the pen of the writer. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the out of Ephraim — a root against Amalek; the Benjamin — among the people; the Machir — governors came down; the Zebulun the scribes — gifts differing by grace (Rom. 12:6-7).

The witnesses agree the song honours the tribes that answered the call — "out of Ephraim... Benjamin... Machir... Zebulun": each is named for the part it bore, even Zebulun's scribes who "handle the pen of the writer" lending their service; the recording of those who came to help shows that God takes note of all who serve his cause, each according to his gift; it teaches that the LORD honours every willing contribution to his work, the warrior and the scribe alike, and that the people of God should each bring what they have, "having then gifts differing according to the grace... let us wait on our ministering" (Rom. 12:6-7), the willing tribes named in the song.

So the witnesses make the verse the willing tribes: The witnesses note the out of Ephraim: a root against Amalek. The witnesses note the Benjamin: among the people. The witnesses note the Machir: governors came down. The witnesses note the Zebulun the scribes: gifts differing by grace (Rom. 12:6-7). The expositors observe the out of Ephraim: a root against Amalek. The expositors observe the Benjamin: among the people. The expositors observe the Machir: governors came down. The expositors observe the Zebulun the scribes: gifts differing by grace (Rom. 12:6-7). The believer is taught to mark the out of Ephraim, the Benjamin, the Machir, the Zebulun the scribes, and to offer himself willingly in the LORD's cause. The verse thus records that the song names Ephraim, Benjamin, Machir, and Zebulun who answered the call — God honouring every willing contribution, the warrior and the scribe alike, each bringing his gift according to the grace given (Rom. 12:6-7).
15:
"And the princes of Issachar were with Deborah; even Issachar, and also Barak... For the divisions of Reuben there were great thoughts of heart." The witnesses note Issachar joining Deborah, but Reuben's divisions and great searchings of heart. The witnesses note that Gill notes the princes of Issachar came to Mount Tabor to offer themselves, the men of that tribe understanding the times. The witnesses note that for the divisions of Reuben there were great thoughts of heart. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Issachar with Deborah — prompt and ready; the understood the times — came to help; the the divisions of Reuben — torn and divided; the great thoughts of heart — a double minded man unstable (James 1:8).

The witnesses agree the song sets the ready Issachar against the hesitating Reuben — "the princes of Issachar were with Deborah... For the divisions of Reuben there were great thoughts of heart": Issachar, who "understood the times," came promptly, while Reuben was torn with "great thoughts" and irresolution, debating whether to come; the contrast between the decisive and the divided shows how indecision keeps men back from God's cause; it teaches that the people of God must not be hindered by the deliberations of a divided heart, but come promptly to his help, "a double minded man is unstable in all his ways" (James 1:8), Issachar with Deborah while Reuben searched his heart.

So the witnesses make the verse Issachar and Reuben: The witnesses note the Issachar with Deborah: prompt and ready. The witnesses note the understood the times: came to help. The witnesses note the the divisions of Reuben: torn and divided. The witnesses note the great thoughts of heart: a double minded man unstable (James 1:8). The expositors observe the Issachar with Deborah: prompt and ready. The expositors observe the understood the times: came to help. The expositors observe the the divisions of Reuben: torn and divided. The expositors observe the great thoughts of heart: a double minded man unstable (James 1:8). The believer is taught to mark the Issachar with Deborah, the understood the times, the the divisions of Reuben, the great thoughts of heart, and to offer himself willingly in the LORD's cause. The verse thus records that Issachar came promptly while Reuben was torn with great searchings of heart — the decisive set against the divided, indecision keeping men back from God's cause, the double minded man unstable in all his ways (James 1:8).
16:
"Why abodest thou among the sheepfolds, to hear the bleatings of the flocks? For the divisions of Reuben there were great searchings of heart." The witnesses note Reuben staying among the sheepfolds, preferring ease to the LORD's cause. The witnesses note that Gill notes this tribe abounded with flocks, and at this critical juncture judged it wisest to attend to their own concerns. The witnesses note that for the divisions of Reuben there were great searchings of heart. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the why abodest thou — among the sheepfolds; the the bleatings of the flocks — their own concerns; the preferring ease — to the cause of God; the entangled in affairs — no soldier entangles himself (2 Tim. 2:4).

The witnesses agree the song gently rebukes Reuben's choice of ease — "Why abodest thou among the sheepfolds, to hear the bleatings of the flocks?": preferring the care of their cattle to the cause of God, they stayed home while their brethren fought; the "great searchings of heart," repeated, mark the inward struggle that ended in inaction; it teaches that the love of ease and earthly business may keep God's people from his service, and that to choose the sheepfold over the battle is a fault, "no man that warreth entangleth himself with the affairs of this life" (2 Tim. 2:4), Reuben abiding among the sheepfolds.

So the witnesses make the verse among the sheepfolds: The witnesses note the why abodest thou: among the sheepfolds. The witnesses note the the bleatings of the flocks: their own concerns. The witnesses note the preferring ease: to the cause of God. The witnesses note the entangled in affairs: no soldier entangles himself (2 Tim. 2:4). The expositors observe the why abodest thou: among the sheepfolds. The expositors observe the the bleatings of the flocks: their own concerns. The expositors observe the preferring ease: to the cause of God. The expositors observe the entangled in affairs: no soldier entangles himself (2 Tim. 2:4). The believer is taught to mark the why abodest thou, the the bleatings of the flocks, the preferring ease, the entangled in affairs, and to offer himself willingly in the LORD's cause. The verse thus records that Reuben abode among the sheepfolds, preferring ease to the LORD's cause — the love of earthly business keeping God's people from his service, no soldier entangling himself with the affairs of this life (2 Tim. 2:4).
17:
"Gilead abode beyond Jordan: and why did Dan remain in ships? Asher continued on the sea shore, and abode in his breaches." The witnesses note Gilead, Dan, and Asher staying away from the war, each for its own occupation. The witnesses note that Gill notes Gilead abode beyond Jordan, and the tribes are here distinguished. The witnesses note that Dan remained in ships and Asher on the sea shore. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Gilead beyond Jordan — staying away; the Dan in ships — at his trade; the Asher on the sea shore — at his harbours; the private before common — look on the things of others (Phil. 2:4).

The witnesses agree the song marks more tribes that held back — "Gilead abode beyond Jordan... Dan remain in ships... Asher continued on the sea shore": each, absorbed in its own trade and dwelling, failed to come to the common cause; the catalogue of the absent shows how many of God's people put their private interests before his service; it teaches that the cause of God claims the help of all his people, and that to stand aloof for one's own occupation, when the battle calls, is a failure of duty, "look not every man on his own things, but every man also on the things of others" (Phil. 2:4), Gilead, Dan, and Asher abiding apart.

So the witnesses make the verse the absent tribes: The witnesses note the Gilead beyond Jordan: staying away. The witnesses note the Dan in ships: at his trade. The witnesses note the Asher on the sea shore: at his harbours. The witnesses note the private before common: look on the things of others (Phil. 2:4). The expositors observe the Gilead beyond Jordan: staying away. The expositors observe the Dan in ships: at his trade. The expositors observe the Asher on the sea shore: at his harbours. The expositors observe the private before common: look on the things of others (Phil. 2:4). The believer is taught to mark the Gilead beyond Jordan, the Dan in ships, the Asher on the sea shore, the private before common, and to offer himself willingly in the LORD's cause. The verse thus records that Gilead, Dan, and Asher stayed away, each at its own occupation — many of God's people putting private interest before his service, called instead to look on the things of others (Phil. 2:4).
18:
"Zebulun and Naphtali were a people that jeoparded their lives unto the death in the high places of the field." The witnesses note Zebulun and Naphtali risking their lives unto death in the battle. The witnesses note that Gill notes these two tribes were chiefly concerned in the war, the 10,000 that followed Barak, who willingly offered themselves and were the most active. The witnesses note that they jeoparded their lives unto the death. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Zebulun and Naphtali — the valiant; the jeoparded their lives — risking all; the unto the death — not counting life dear; the the noblest service — they loved not their lives unto the death (Rev. 12:11).

The witnesses agree the highest praise is given to the bravest tribes — "Zebulun and Naphtali... jeoparded their lives unto the death in the high places of the field": these were the valiant who risked all, not counting their lives dear, in the cause of God; the contrast with the tribes that held back is sharp, and the honour given to those who ventured all is great; it teaches that the noblest service to God is to hazard all for his cause, loving not our lives unto the death, "they loved not their lives unto the death" (Rev. 12:11); the willing and valiant who jeopard their lives are honoured above all, Zebulun and Naphtali risking death for the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse jeoparded their lives: The witnesses note the Zebulun and Naphtali: the valiant. The witnesses note the jeoparded their lives: risking all. The witnesses note the unto the death: not counting life dear. The witnesses note the the noblest service: they loved not their lives unto the death (Rev. 12:11). The expositors observe the Zebulun and Naphtali: the valiant. The expositors observe the jeoparded their lives: risking all. The expositors observe the unto the death: not counting life dear. The expositors observe the the noblest service: they loved not their lives unto the death (Rev. 12:11). The believer is taught to mark the Zebulun and Naphtali, the jeoparded their lives, the unto the death, the the noblest service, and to offer himself willingly in the LORD's cause. The verse thus records that Zebulun and Naphtali jeoparded their lives unto death in the battle — the noblest service to hazard all for God's cause, the valiant honoured above all, they loved not their lives unto the death (Rev. 12:11).
19:
"The kings came and fought, then fought the kings of Canaan in Taanach by the waters of Megiddo; they took no gain of money." The witnesses note the kings of Canaan fighting at Taanach, yet taking no gain of money. The witnesses note that Gill notes the kings that came with Sisera, fighting near the same place where Joshua had fought. The witnesses note that they took no gain of money. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the kings came — and fought; the the kings of Canaan — at Taanach; the came for plunder — their greed; the took no gain — the expectation of the wicked perishes (Prov. 10:28).

The witnesses agree the song notes the confederate kings who fought — "the kings came and fought... the kings of Canaan in Taanach... they took no gain of money": the mighty array of kings came for plunder, yet gained nothing, their hope of spoil utterly disappointed; the frustrating of the enemy's greed shows how God turns the expectations of the wicked to nothing; it teaches that the schemes of God's enemies for gain come to naught, their covetous designs frustrated, "the expectation of the wicked shall perish" (Prov. 10:28), the kings of Canaan taking no gain of money.

So the witnesses make the verse no gain of money: The witnesses note the the kings came: and fought. The witnesses note the the kings of Canaan: at Taanach. The witnesses note the came for plunder: their greed. The witnesses note the took no gain: the expectation of the wicked perishes (Prov. 10:28). The expositors observe the the kings came: and fought. The expositors observe the the kings of Canaan: at Taanach. The expositors observe the came for plunder: their greed. The expositors observe the took no gain: the expectation of the wicked perishes (Prov. 10:28). The believer is taught to mark the the kings came, the the kings of Canaan, the came for plunder, the took no gain, and to offer himself willingly in the LORD's cause. The verse thus records that the kings of Canaan fought yet took no gain of money — God turning the greedy expectations of the wicked to nothing, their covetous designs frustrated, the expectation of the wicked perishing (Prov. 10:28).
20:
"They fought from heaven; the stars in their courses fought against Sisera." The witnesses note that the very heavens fought for Israel — the stars in their courses against Sisera. The witnesses note that Gill notes either the angels of heaven, or the heavens and elements, fought against Sisera, a violent storm of rain and hail discomfiting his army. The witnesses note that the stars in their courses fought against Sisera. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the they fought from heaven — the elements; the the stars in their courses — god's host; the against Sisera — the storm; the creation his army — the LORD thundered and discomfited (1 Sam. 7:10).

The witnesses agree the victory is ascribed to heaven itself — "They fought from heaven; the stars in their courses fought against Sisera": Gill notes a great storm of rain and hail, the very elements of heaven, discomfited Sisera's host; God turned the powers of nature into his army against the foe; it teaches that the LORD commands all creation to fight for his people, the heavens themselves arrayed against his enemies, "the LORD thundered with a great thunder... upon the Philistines, and discomfited them" (1 Sam. 7:10); the stars and storms are his host, and all creation serves his redemptive ends, the heavens fighting against Sisera.

So the witnesses make the verse fought from heaven: The witnesses note the they fought from heaven: the elements. The witnesses note the the stars in their courses: God's host. The witnesses note the against Sisera: the storm. The witnesses note the creation his army: the LORD thundered and discomfited (1 Sam. 7:10). The expositors observe the they fought from heaven: the elements. The expositors observe the the stars in their courses: God's host. The expositors observe the against Sisera: the storm. The expositors observe the creation his army: the LORD thundered and discomfited (1 Sam. 7:10). The believer is taught to mark the they fought from heaven, the the stars in their courses, the against Sisera, the creation his army, and to offer himself willingly in the LORD's cause. The verse thus records that the heavens fought for Israel, the stars in their courses against Sisera — the LORD commanding all creation to fight for his people, the storm his army, as he thundered and discomfited the Philistines (1 Sam. 7:10).
21:
"The river of Kishon swept them away, that ancient river, the river Kishon. O my soul, thou hast trodden down strength." The witnesses note the river Kishon sweeping away Sisera's host, and Deborah's soul treading down strength. The witnesses note that Gill notes Sisera's army was drawn to Kishon, and many in their confusion were swept away and drowned. The witnesses note that Deborah says, O my soul, thou hast trodden down strength. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the river of Kishon — swept them away; the that ancient river — the torrent; the trodden down strength — the foe broken; the the waters his instrument — the LORD mightier than the waves (Ps. 93:4).

The witnesses agree the flood completed the rout — "the river of Kishon swept them away, that ancient river": the swollen torrent, fed by the storm, drowned the fleeing host, as the Red Sea had drowned Pharaoh; Deborah's soul triumphs, "thou hast trodden down strength," over the might of the enemy now broken; it teaches that God uses the very waters as his instrument of judgment, sweeping away the proud, and that his people may triumph over the trodden-down strength of their foes, "the LORD on high is mightier than... the mighty waves of the sea" (Ps. 93:4); the ancient Kishon swept the host away, and the soul of the redeemed trod down strength.

So the witnesses make the verse Kishon swept them away: The witnesses note the the river of Kishon: swept them away. The witnesses note the that ancient river: the torrent. The witnesses note the trodden down strength: the foe broken. The witnesses note the the waters his instrument: the LORD mightier than the waves (Ps. 93:4). The expositors observe the the river of Kishon: swept them away. The expositors observe the that ancient river: the torrent. The expositors observe the trodden down strength: the foe broken. The expositors observe the the waters his instrument: the LORD mightier than the waves (Ps. 93:4). The believer is taught to mark the the river of Kishon, the that ancient river, the trodden down strength, the the waters his instrument, and to offer himself willingly in the LORD's cause. The verse thus records that the river Kishon swept away Sisera's host, and Deborah's soul trod down strength — God using the very waters as his instrument of judgment, sweeping away the proud, the LORD mightier than the mighty waves of the sea (Ps. 93:4).
22:
"Then were the horsehoofs broken by the means of the pransings, the pransings of their mighty ones." The witnesses note the horses' hoofs broken in the headlong flight of Sisera's mighty ones. The witnesses note that Gill notes the hoofs were broken through the force of the waters, or through the swift haste they made to run away. The witnesses note that this was by the prancings of their mighty ones. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the horsehoofs broken — in the flight; the the prancings — the headlong rout; the their mighty ones — the boasted strength; the the horse a vain thing — a vain thing for safety (Ps. 33:17).

The witnesses agree the rout is pictured in the broken hoofs of the fleeing cavalry — "the horsehoofs broken by the means of the pransings": the proud chariots and horses, Sisera's boasted strength, were ruined in their headlong flight; the very might in which the foe trusted became the means of his destruction; it teaches that the strength of the wicked fails them in the day of God's judgment, the horse a vain thing for safety, "an horse is a vain thing for safety: neither shall he deliver any by his great strength" (Ps. 33:17), the horse-hoofs broken in the flight.

So the witnesses make the verse the horse-hoofs broken: The witnesses note the the horsehoofs broken: in the flight. The witnesses note the the prancings: the headlong rout. The witnesses note the their mighty ones: the boasted strength. The witnesses note the the horse a vain thing: a vain thing for safety (Ps. 33:17). The expositors observe the the horsehoofs broken: in the flight. The expositors observe the the prancings: the headlong rout. The expositors observe the their mighty ones: the boasted strength. The expositors observe the the horse a vain thing: a vain thing for safety (Ps. 33:17). The believer is taught to mark the the horsehoofs broken, the the prancings, the their mighty ones, the the horse a vain thing, and to love the LORD and shine as the sun. The verse thus records that the horse-hoofs were broken in the headlong flight — the strength in which the foe trusted failing in the day of judgment, the horse a vain thing for safety, delivering none by his great strength (Ps. 33:17).
23:
"Curse ye Meroz, said the angel of the LORD, curse ye bitterly the inhabitants thereof; because they came not to the help of the LORD, to the help of the LORD against the mighty." The witnesses note the curse pronounced on Meroz for not coming to the help of the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes Deborah said this under a spirit of prophecy, not from her own revengeful spirit, but from the Spirit of God, or suggested by an angel. The witnesses note that they came not to the help of the LORD against the mighty. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the curse ye Meroz — the neutral; the the angel of the LORD — by God's word; the came not to help — stood aloof; the no neutral ground — he that is not with me is against me (Matt. 12:30).

The witnesses agree the song turns from blessing the willing to cursing the neutral — "Curse ye Meroz... because they came not to the help of the LORD": those who stood aloof in the day of battle, neither helping nor hindering, are cursed by the word of God; the sharpness of the curse on mere neutrality shows that in God's cause there is no neutral ground; it teaches that to fail to help the LORD when his cause calls is itself a grievous fault, that lukewarmness and non-involvement are condemned, "he that is not with me is against me" (Matt. 12:30); the curse on Meroz warns all who hold back from the help of the LORD against the mighty.

So the witnesses make the verse curse ye Meroz: The witnesses note the curse ye Meroz: the neutral. The witnesses note the the angel of the LORD: by God's word. The witnesses note the came not to help: stood aloof. The witnesses note the no neutral ground: he that is not with me is against me (Matt. 12:30). The expositors observe the curse ye Meroz: the neutral. The expositors observe the the angel of the LORD: by God's word. The expositors observe the came not to help: stood aloof. The expositors observe the no neutral ground: he that is not with me is against me (Matt. 12:30). The believer is taught to mark the curse ye Meroz, the the angel of the LORD, the came not to help, the no neutral ground, and to love the LORD and shine as the sun. The verse thus records that Meroz was cursed for not coming to the help of the LORD — no neutral ground in God's cause, lukewarmness and non-involvement condemned, he that is not with me is against me (Matt. 12:30).
24:
"Blessed above women shall Jael the wife of Heber the Kenite be, blessed shall she be above women in the tent." The witnesses note Jael pronounced blessed above women for her part in the deliverance. The witnesses note that Gill notes Jael is blessed under the same prophetic influence that Meroz was cursed, the one for not helping Israel, the other for doing it. The witnesses note that she is blessed above women in the tent. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the blessed above women — shall Jael be; the the wife of Heber — the Kenite; the set against Meroz — blessing for curse; the honoured for acting — blessed are they that hunger for righteousness (Matt. 5:6).

The witnesses agree the song sets the blessing of Jael against the curse of Meroz — "Blessed above women shall Jael... be": where Meroz failed to help and was cursed, Jael, by her bold act, served the cause of God and is blessed; Gill notes the blessing is pronounced under the same prophetic Spirit; the honouring of Jael for her deed teaches that God owns and blesses those who, in his providence, become the instruments of his judgment on his enemies; the contrast of curse and blessing shows the difference between standing aloof and acting for God, "blessed are they which... hunger and thirst after righteousness" (cf. Matt. 5:6), Jael blessed above women for her part.

So the witnesses make the verse blessed above women: The witnesses note the blessed above women: shall Jael be. The witnesses note the the wife of Heber: the Kenite. The witnesses note the set against Meroz: blessing for curse. The witnesses note the honoured for acting: blessed are they that hunger for righteousness (Matt. 5:6). The expositors observe the blessed above women: shall Jael be. The expositors observe the the wife of Heber: the Kenite. The expositors observe the set against Meroz: blessing for curse. The expositors observe the honoured for acting: blessed are they that hunger for righteousness (Matt. 5:6). The believer is taught to mark the blessed above women, the the wife of Heber, the set against Meroz, the honoured for acting, and to love the LORD and shine as the sun. The verse thus records that Jael was pronounced blessed above women — set against the cursed Meroz, God owning those who serve his cause where others stand aloof, the contrast of acting for God and holding back (Matt. 5:6).
25:
"He asked water, and she gave him milk; she brought forth butter in a lordly dish." The witnesses note Jael giving Sisera milk and butter, a show of hospitality. The witnesses note that Gill notes Sisera asked it of her when he turned into her tent. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set her bringing butter in a lordly dish. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he asked water — the weary fugitive; the she gave him milk — more than asked; the butter in a lordly dish — generous show; the the prelude to judgment — sudden destruction in their peace (1 Thess. 5:3).

The witnesses agree the song marks Jael's seeming kindness — "He asked water, and she gave him milk... butter in a lordly dish": she met the weary fugitive with more than he asked, a show of generous hospitality; yet this very welcome was the prelude to the judgment that fell on the oppressor of God's people; the giving of milk for water, exceeding his request, lulled him to the security in which he met his end; it teaches that the providence of God may use even common courtesies to bring his purposes to pass, the proud enemy disarmed by seeming kindness before the stroke of judgment, "when they shall say, Peace and safety; then sudden destruction cometh" (1 Thess. 5:3), Jael giving Sisera milk in a lordly dish.

So the witnesses make the verse milk in a lordly dish: The witnesses note the he asked water: the weary fugitive. The witnesses note the she gave him milk: more than asked. The witnesses note the butter in a lordly dish: generous show. The witnesses note the the prelude to judgment: sudden destruction in their peace (1 Thess. 5:3). The expositors observe the he asked water: the weary fugitive. The expositors observe the she gave him milk: more than asked. The expositors observe the butter in a lordly dish: generous show. The expositors observe the the prelude to judgment: sudden destruction in their peace (1 Thess. 5:3). The believer is taught to mark the he asked water, the she gave him milk, the butter in a lordly dish, the the prelude to judgment, and to love the LORD and shine as the sun. The verse thus records that Jael gave Sisera milk and butter, exceeding his request — the seeming kindness the prelude to judgment, the proud enemy lulled to security before the stroke, sudden destruction coming on their peace (1 Thess. 5:3).
26:
"She put her hand to the nail, and her right hand to the workmen's hammer; and with the hammer she smote Sisera, she smote off his head, when she had pierced and stricken through his temples." The witnesses note Jael taking the tent-nail and hammer and slaying Sisera. The witnesses note that Gill notes she took up a pin from her tent, with which it was fastened to the ground, and clapped it to the temples of Sisera. The witnesses note that she pierced and stricken through his temples. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the her hand to the nail — the tent-peg; the the workmen's hammer — humble implements; the she smote Sisera — the captain fell; the the mighty brought low — god chose the weak things (1 Cor. 1:27).

The witnesses agree the deed is told in vivid stroke — "she put her hand to the nail... with the hammer she smote Sisera... pierced and stricken through his temples": the humble implements of the tent, the nail and the hammer, became in her hand the instruments of God's judgment on the oppressor; the great captain of nine hundred chariots fell by a woman's hand and a tent-peg; it teaches that God brings down the mighty by the meanest means, the proud foe slain by a tent-nail, that the glory might be his alone, "God hath chosen the weak things of the world to confound the things which are mighty" (1 Cor. 1:27), Jael smiting Sisera with the hammer and nail.

So the witnesses make the verse the nail and hammer: The witnesses note the her hand to the nail: the tent-peg. The witnesses note the the workmen's hammer: humble implements. The witnesses note the she smote Sisera: the captain fell. The witnesses note the the mighty brought low: God chose the weak things (1 Cor. 1:27). The expositors observe the her hand to the nail: the tent-peg. The expositors observe the the workmen's hammer: humble implements. The expositors observe the she smote Sisera: the captain fell. The expositors observe the the mighty brought low: God chose the weak things (1 Cor. 1:27). The believer is taught to mark the her hand to the nail, the the workmen's hammer, the she smote Sisera, the the mighty brought low, and to love the LORD and shine as the sun. The verse thus records that Jael slew Sisera with the tent-nail and hammer — God bringing down the mighty by the meanest means, the proud captain slain by a woman's hand and a tent-peg, that the glory might be his, choosing the weak to confound the mighty (1 Cor. 1:27).
27:
"At her feet he bowed, he fell, he lay down: at her feet he bowed, he fell: where he bowed, there he fell down dead." The witnesses note Sisera falling dead at Jael's feet, the repeated words marking his utter overthrow. The witnesses note that Gill notes at her first stroke she had done the business so effectually that he dropped at once. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the threefold falling, marking his death. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the at her feet — he bowed; the he fell, he lay down — the threefold fall; the where he bowed — there he fell; the the proud brought low — they are fallen, but we stand upright (Ps. 20:8).

The witnesses agree the threefold repetition drives home the completeness of his ruin — "he bowed, he fell, he lay down... where he bowed, there he fell down dead": the great oppressor, who had ground Israel twenty years, lay dead at the feet of a woman; the dwelling on his fall marks how the proud are utterly brought low; it teaches that the LORD lays the mightiest of his enemies in the dust, the oppressor fallen never to rise, his end sudden and complete, "they are brought down and fallen: but we are risen, and stand upright" (Ps. 20:8), Sisera fallen down dead at Jael's feet.

So the witnesses make the verse there he fell down dead: The witnesses note the at her feet: he bowed. The witnesses note the he fell, he lay down: the threefold fall. The witnesses note the where he bowed: there he fell. The witnesses note the the proud brought low: they are fallen, but we stand upright (Ps. 20:8). The expositors observe the at her feet: he bowed. The expositors observe the he fell, he lay down: the threefold fall. The expositors observe the where he bowed: there he fell. The expositors observe the the proud brought low: they are fallen, but we stand upright (Ps. 20:8). The believer is taught to mark the at her feet, the he fell, he lay down, the where he bowed, the the proud brought low, and to love the LORD and shine as the sun. The verse thus records that Sisera fell dead at Jael's feet, the repeated words marking his utter overthrow — the LORD laying the mightiest enemy in the dust, the oppressor fallen never to rise, they are brought down and fallen, but we stand upright (Ps. 20:8).
28:
"The mother of Sisera looked out at a window, and cried through the lattice, Why is his chariot so long in coming? why tarry the wheels of his chariots?" The witnesses note Sisera's mother watching at the window for her son's return. The witnesses note that Gill notes she looked toward the high road, expecting Sisera to return in his chariot with his victorious army. The witnesses note that she cried, Why is his chariot so long in coming? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the mother of Sisera — watched; the at a window — for her son; the why so long in coming — the vain watching; the the wicked's hope vain — the triumphing of the wicked is short (Job 20:5).

The witnesses agree the song turns to a scene of pathos and irony — "the mother of Sisera looked out at a window... Why is his chariot so long in coming?": the anxious mother watches for the son who lies dead, her hope of his triumph already turned to ruin; the picture of her vain watching, ignorant of his fall, shows the emptiness of the wicked's expectations; it teaches that the hopes of the ungodly are built on sand and end in disappointment, the proud confidence of the enemy mocked by the event, "the triumphing of the wicked is short" (Job 20:5), Sisera's mother watching in vain at the window.

So the witnesses make the verse looked out at a window: The witnesses note the the mother of Sisera: watched. The witnesses note the at a window: for her son. The witnesses note the why so long in coming: the vain watching. The witnesses note the the wicked's hope vain: the triumphing of the wicked is short (Job 20:5). The expositors observe the the mother of Sisera: watched. The expositors observe the at a window: for her son. The expositors observe the why so long in coming: the vain watching. The expositors observe the the wicked's hope vain: the triumphing of the wicked is short (Job 20:5). The believer is taught to mark the the mother of Sisera, the at a window, the why so long in coming, the the wicked's hope vain, and to love the LORD and shine as the sun. The verse thus records that Sisera's mother watched in vain for the son who lay dead — the emptiness of the wicked's expectations, their hopes built on sand and ending in disappointment, the triumphing of the wicked short (Job 20:5).
29:
"Her wise ladies answered her, yea, she returned answer to herself," The witnesses note the wise ladies answering Sisera's mother, and she answering herself, to allay her fear. The witnesses note that Gill notes her ladies, or maids of honour, each in turn endeavoured to comfort her and make her easy. The witnesses note that she returned answer to herself. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the her wise ladies — answered her; the she returned answer — to herself; the false comfort — imagined reasons; the peace where none — peace, peace, when there is no peace (Jer. 6:14).

The witnesses agree the mother and her ladies sought to quiet their growing fear with hopeful guesses — "her wise ladies answered her, yea, she returned answer to herself": they comforted themselves with imagined reasons for the delay, refusing to face the dreadful truth; the self-soothing of the anxious heart with false hopes shows how men deceive themselves in the day of coming judgment; it teaches that the ungodly often flatter themselves with vain comforts when ruin is at the door, crying peace where there is no peace, "they have healed the hurt of the daughter of my people slightly, saying, Peace, peace; when there is no peace" (Jer. 6:14), the ladies answering with false comfort.

So the witnesses make the verse answered herself: The witnesses note the her wise ladies: answered her. The witnesses note the she returned answer: to herself. The witnesses note the false comfort: imagined reasons. The witnesses note the peace where none: peace, peace, when there is no peace (Jer. 6:14). The expositors observe the her wise ladies: answered her. The expositors observe the she returned answer: to herself. The expositors observe the false comfort: imagined reasons. The expositors observe the peace where none: peace, peace, when there is no peace (Jer. 6:14). The believer is taught to mark the her wise ladies, the she returned answer, the false comfort, the peace where none, and to love the LORD and shine as the sun. The verse thus records that the ladies and the mother soothed themselves with hopeful guesses — the self-deceiving of the anxious heart with false comforts, the ungodly crying peace where there is no peace (Jer. 6:14).
30:
"Have they not sped? have they not divided the prey; to every man a damsel or two; to Sisera a prey of divers colours, a prey of divers colours of needlework..." The witnesses note the mother imagining her son delayed by dividing the rich spoil. The witnesses note that Gill notes she supposed they had found the enemy and the spoil, and were dividing it. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the imagined prey, a damsel or two to every man. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the have they not sped — her fancy; the divided the prey — captive women; the a prey of divers colours — rich spoil; the the covetous dream — this night thy soul shall be required (Luke 12:20).

The witnesses agree the mother's fancy paints a scene of victorious plunder — "have they not divided the prey... to Sisera a prey of divers colours": she imagines her son delayed by the sharing of spoil and captive women, dreaming of triumph while he lies dead; the contrast between her gorgeous imaginings and the grim reality is the sharpest irony of the song; it teaches the vanity of the dreams of the covetous and proud, who count their spoil before it is won, the prey divided in fancy that they shall never touch, "thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee" (Luke 12:20), the mother dreaming of a prey of divers colours.

So the witnesses make the verse divided the prey: The witnesses note the have they not sped: her fancy. The witnesses note the divided the prey: captive women. The witnesses note the a prey of divers colours: rich spoil. The witnesses note the the covetous dream: this night thy soul shall be required (Luke 12:20). The expositors observe the have they not sped: her fancy. The expositors observe the divided the prey: captive women. The expositors observe the a prey of divers colours: rich spoil. The expositors observe the the covetous dream: this night thy soul shall be required (Luke 12:20). The believer is taught to mark the have they not sped, the divided the prey, the a prey of divers colours, the the covetous dream, and to love the LORD and shine as the sun. The verse thus records that the mother imagined her son delayed by dividing the rich spoil — the vanity of the dreams of the proud and covetous, the prey divided in fancy that they shall never touch, thou fool, this night thy soul shall be required of thee (Luke 12:20).
31:
"So let all thine enemies perish, O LORD: but let them that love him be as the sun when he goeth forth in his might. And the land had rest forty years." The witnesses note the closing benediction — the enemies perish, the lovers of God shine as the sun — and the land's forty years' rest. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is not only a wish but a prophecy that so it would be: the enemies perish, the lovers of God shine. The witnesses note that Ellicott sets the closing prayer and the rest of the land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the let thine enemies perish — their certain end; the them that love him — his friends; the as the sun — shining in glory; the the land had rest — the righteous shine as the sun (Matt. 13:43).

The witnesses agree the song ends with a great prophetic benediction, setting the two destinies side by side — "let all thine enemies perish, O LORD: but let them that love him be as the sun when he goeth forth in his might": the foes of God shall come to ruin, as Sisera did, while those who love him shall rise in ever-growing glory like the sun; Gill marks it a prophecy as well as a prayer; it teaches the certain end of God's enemies and the shining destiny of his friends, "then shall the righteous shine forth as the sun in the kingdom of their Father" (Matt. 13:43); and the chapter closes with the rest the deliverance brought, the land at rest forty years, the lovers of God shining as the sun.

So the witnesses make the verse as the sun: The witnesses note the let thine enemies perish: their certain end. The witnesses note the them that love him: his friends. The witnesses note the as the sun: shining in glory. The witnesses note the the land had rest: the righteous shine as the sun (Matt. 13:43). The expositors observe the let thine enemies perish: their certain end. The expositors observe the them that love him: his friends. The expositors observe the as the sun: shining in glory. The expositors observe the the land had rest: the righteous shine as the sun (Matt. 13:43). The believer is taught to mark the let thine enemies perish, the them that love him, the as the sun, the the land had rest, and to love the LORD and shine as the sun. The verse thus records that the song closes with the enemies perishing and the lovers of God shining as the sun, and the land at rest forty years — the certain end of God's enemies and the shining destiny of his friends, the righteous shining forth as the sun in the kingdom of their Father (Matt. 13:43).
